column29: Organisational monitoring of employees’ personal social media activity can be considered fair when it is transparent, proportionate, and clearly connected to legitimate work-related concerns such as protecting the organisation’s reputation or preventing misconduct. Employees should be informed that monitoring may occur, and it should respect reasonable expectations of privacy by avoiding unnecessary scrutiny of purely personal content. However, monitoring becomes unfair when it is done without notice, intrudes excessively into employees’ private lives, is applied selectively or discriminatorily, or punishes lawful personal opinions expressed outside of work that do not affect the organisation.
column30: It is appropriate for an employer to intervene or take disciplinary action when an employee’s personal social media activity clearly affects the workplace, the organisation’s reputation, or the safety and wellbeing of others. This may include situations where posts involve harassment, discrimination, threats, disclosure of confidential information, or behaviour that directly damages trust in the organisation. However, employers should be cautious about intervening in cases where the activity is purely personal, lawful, and unrelated to the employee’s role or the organisation.
column31: Organisations can make social media monitoring policies feel more fair and legitimate by clearly communicating the policy to employees, explaining why monitoring may be necessary, and outlining what types of activity may lead to intervention. Involving employees in developing the policy, applying it consistently across the organisation, and ensuring that monitoring is proportionate and respectful of privacy can also help build trust and transparency.

column29:
Fair - if the employee is posting content in an easily identifiable work uniform or recording content in a work setting during work hours

Unfair - if the employee is sharing/reposting/posting opinions or thoughts (political or otherwise) that don’t actively impact the company/organisation. 

I don’t believe in scrupulous monitoring of employees social media in general, if an employee draws attention to their social media by posting something that directly affects the company then fair enough if they want to go through it to check if there are any other posts affecting the company or recorded during work etc.

Overall; employees should be conscious of what they post but management shouldn’t go out of their way to monitor their employees if there’s no reason for it. On the other hand, social media is widely accessible and if their accounts are not private, anyone can see what they post which leaves them vulnerable to scrutiny by management.
